title: Global Control with the Tavis-Cummings Interaction
abstract: We study the controllability of a system of qubits under global control, where control pulses act identically on all qubits. Specifically, we consider a collection of qubits identically coupled to a single bosonic mode, or harmonic oscillator, via the Jaynes-Cummings interaction. This collective coupling, known as the Tavis-Cummings (TC) interaction, has been realized in several quantum computing platforms, including superconducting and atomic qubit systems. Although the qubits do not interact directly with one another, they can become entangled through their common coupling to the bosonic mode. We characterize the group of unitaries that can be implemented on the joint Hilbert space of the qubits and bosonic mode using the TC interaction together with a global $z$ field $J_z$, corresponding to identical z rotations on all qubits. We show that for n>2 qubits the set of realizable unitaries is restricted by an "accidental" symmetry of the TC Hamiltonian, distinct from its "standard" U(1) and permutational symmetries. On the other hand, we find that the Hamiltonian $J_z^2$ breaks this accidental symmetry and, together with the TC interaction and $J_z$, achieves semi-universality: it allows the implementation of arbitrary unitaries that respect permutational and U(1) symmetry, up to certain constraints on the center of the group. In a companion paper, we further analyze this remarkable accidental symmetry and show that it can be understood through Schwinger's bosonic model of angular momentum.
summary: The paper rigorously investigates the controllability of qubits coupled via a Tavis-Cummings interaction to a bosonic mode. It demonstrates that while initial controls are limited by an 'accidental' symmetry, introducing a secondary global field ($J_z^2$) breaks this constraint, enabling near-universal control over the system's unitary operations.
main_problem: The core problem is characterizing the group of unitaries implementable on a joint qubit-bosonic system using global control fields derived from the Tavis-Cummings interaction.
main_result: The realizable unitary group is restricted by an 'accidental' symmetry when only the TC interaction and $J_z$ are used, but adding $J_z^2$ breaks this symmetry, achieving semi-universality for implementing arbitrary unitaries respecting key symmetries.
method: The analysis relies heavily on Lie algebra theory to characterize the generated groups of unitaries by analyzing commutator subalgebras $[\mathfrak{g}, \mathfrak{g}]$ and comparing them to the full symmetry group $	ext{VU}(1)	imes	ext{Sn}$.
model_or_system: The system consists of $n$ qubits identically coupled via the Tavis-Cummings (TC) interaction to a single bosonic mode (harmonic oscillator). Control is applied globally using $J_z$ and potentially $J_z^2$.
key_observables: The group of realizable unitaries ($V_z$), the structure of the Lie algebra ($\mathfrak{v}_z, \mathfrak{w}_z$), and the dimension/inclusion relationships between commutator subalgebras.
important_parameters: Number of qubits ($n$), coupling strengths ($g_{TC}$), and the presence/absence of additional symmetry-breaking terms like $J_z^2$.
main_assumptions: The analysis often assumes that the bosonic mode acts as an ancillary system initialized in a fixed energy eigenstate, returning to it at the end.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate schematic relationships between Lie algebras generated by combinations of Hamiltonians and show how commutator subalgebras reduce or expand based on added terms.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from characterizing the initial restricted group $V_z$ using $\{	ext{iHTC}, 	ext{iJz}\}$, to showing that adding $	ext{iJ}_2^z$ breaks an accidental symmetry, leading to semi-universality. Later sections delve into detailed Lie algebra decompositions and structural proofs.
why_interesting: This work provides rigorous mathematical tools for determining the fundamental limits of quantum control in complex, interacting many-body systems, which is crucial for designing fault-tolerant quantum computation protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. First main result (Theorem 1 and Theorem 7). Schematic relationship between different Lie algebras generated by various subsets of HTC ∝(J+a + J−a†), HTC ∝i(J+a −J−a†), Jz, J2 z , and a†a. All these algebras are subalgebras of g, the Lie algebra of all PI, U(1)-invariant Hamiltonians. Although the algebras svz, vz, wz, v+ z , and g are all distinct, after removing their centers, i.e., after passing to their commutator subalgebras, they reduce to only two possibilities: [g, g] = [v+ z , v+ z ] and svz = [vz, vz] = [wz, wz]. The latter algebra is restricted by the accidental symmetry of HTC, which, to the best of our knowledge, has not been identified before. The centers removed by...

FIG. 2. Second main result (Theorem 8). The goal is to implement a unitary U on the qubits by coupling them to a bosonic mode initial- ized in the eigenstate |k⟩of its intrinsic Hamiltonian a†a, with eigen- value k. After the operation, the bosonic mode returns to the same pure state and hence remains unentangled with the qubits. This is achieved by implementing a unitary V using the Hamiltonians HTC and Jz. The phase eiα denotes the freedom to choose an overall global phase; see Eq. (142). Theorem 8 determines which unitary transformations U can be realized in this way.
FIG. 3. Qubit-Oscillator SWAP unitary. See Eq. (96).

FIG. 4. General accidental symmetry pairs: The matrix elements of HTC are identical in the two illustrated sectors, provided that (q, q′) and (j, j′) satisfy Eq. (118). Note that in general, these sec- tors appear with nontrivial multiplicities within (C2)⊗n ⊗L2(R). Figure from [35].

FIG. 5. Sketch of the proof of Lemma 10. Let F be a PI, U(1)-invariant operator supported only on the subspace spanned by |k⟩and |k + 1⟩, shown in the orange box. Now consider a pair of sectors Hq,j and Hq′,j′ that are related by the accidental symmetry. This symmetry maps the projection of F onto Hq,j to an operator acting on Hq′,j′, and vice versa; these symmetry-transformed operators are shown in purple. The key point is that these symmetry-transformed operators have no support on the subspace spanned by |k⟩and |k + 1⟩.
